title: Negation in Mwotlap
introduction: In grammar, negation is the way we say “no” or “not” in a sentence (for example, in English we add not to a verb: I do not eat; or use no by itself: No!). Mwotlap expresses negation with different forms depending on the type of clause and predicate. Most realis verbs and adjectives use a discontinuous circumfix et…te. In past or counterfactual contexts, the preverbal marker tit replaces et…te. Prohibitive (negative imperative) uses tog or nitog. Existential clauses (“there is no…”) use tateh before the noun. Finally, the fixed particle óòó (or òó) alone means “No.” In this lesson, you will learn each device with clear examples.

example.description: Verbal negation with the circumfix et…te around the verb wuywuy “cough.”

description.description: In realis clauses (present or general statements), negate verbs and adjectival predicates by wrapping the predicate with et (before) and te (after).

example.target_sentence: nēk tit gen liwo vēste en, togtō nēk tit gom vēste qele anen
example.source_sentence: If you hadn’t eaten so much, you wouldn’t have gotten sick.
example.description: Counterfactual past negation: the preverbal marker tit replaces et…te in both the protasis and apodosis (with togtō in the second clause).

description.description: In past or hypothetical (“if…would…”) contexts, use the preverbal marker tit before the verb instead of et, and often pair it with te or a variant form.

example.description: Prohibitive negation with the negative imperative marker nitog (variant tog) before the verb dēmdēm “worry.”

description.description: To form a negative command or prohibition, place tog or its variant nitog before the verb.

example.description: Existential negation: the particle tateh precedes the noun lavēt “celebration.”

description.description: For “there is no…” statements, use the particle tateh before the noun phrase, often with se “anymore/at present” in extended contexts.

example.description: The initial word óòó is a fixed negative answer meaning “No.”

description.description: As an answer to a yes/no question, Mwotlap uses the particle óòó (or variant òó) alone to mean “No.”

conclusion: Remember these five negation devices in Mwotlap: 1) et…te around predicates for realis negation; 2) tit (plus te) for past or counterfactual clauses; 3) tog/nitog before the verb for prohibitions; 4) tateh before a noun for existential negation; and 5) the particle óòó/òó by itself to answer “No.”

translation_drills.target: nēk tit gen liwo vēste en, togtō nēk tit gom vēste qele anen
translation_drills.source: If you hadn’t eaten so much, you wouldn’t have gotten sick.
